chunks.text: Hi. Thanks. You guys are the tail end of our estimates. Not the tail end of our meeting, but the tail end of our estimates hearings. We like to do a little loose end tying up after hearing from our ministers, any hangover questions, of which there are. I just wonder, given that we've sort of got a variety of markets, whether you want to do a little introductory remark before we hand over- Perhaps if I do some-... to the minister.

I'll try and keep it really- Yeah, that would be great... thank you. Look, good afternoon. Long time no see. First of all, just introduce Anna Clark, who's our Deputy Secretary for Labor Science, and Enterprise. She's just been appointed in the last couple of months. And Ingrid Baylis, who's currently acting in Immigration, but has been appointed to our service delivery area for everything non-immigration. So she'll start in that shortly.

And Deputy Secretary for Immigration will be Andy Milne, who's currently at ACC, so he starts with us very shortly. So there's quite a bit of change. These are faces that you will see in the committee. Okay. But there's obviously the biometric capability might be one that you want to come back to. But I did want to just highlight there are a few other areas that you may want to touch on as well.

Yeah. In Immigration, obviously from a policy perspective, there's been a lot of change and a lot of implementation of that. We've really also been trying to keep it in touch with our processing times. When we last met in, I think it was January, things were in a really good place. Things are still in a pretty good place, although AWV and students aren't quite where they were. We're doing our best to get those back on track.

We're happy to talk more about that. We've also got one more visa onto ADEPT since then, family of a temp worker, so that program is continuing to get new visas onto that platform. Workplace Relations, this morning you heard about a lot of legislative change. From employment services side, there's been quite a lot of increase in demand there.

But we have managed to maintain our service quality, in terms of timeliness and customer satisfaction. But happy to talk about the inspectorate and dispute resolution. And then ACC, our role there is really in the policy role, and our main role over the last while has been supporting the turnaround plan.

So that's just some of the areas that I'm very happy to talk to. But over to you, Madam Chair. Great. Thank you very much. We have an opening question from Carl Bates. Thanks, Chair, and thanks for coming back today, and appreciate it's been a bit of a challenging week. I'm interested to dig more into this biometric system, as you will appreciate. So as I understand it, we're writing off, as a government, $31.2 million for this system which doesn't work.

Immigration went to the minister to try and get the cap of funding for whole of life increased, in March last year. That didn't happen, but you've still been able to keep the cap, or the costs within the cap, through some in and out transfers, described in the report as creative accounting.

Can you give us detail on what transfers were made to avoid it having to go back to Cabinet? So just to clarify the numbers, you're correct, 31.2 is the amount that's been written off. The total cost of the project was 33.4. The difference is operating. So there was a small amount of operating expenditure, but the majority was capital, just to clarify that.

Okay. So the overall amount was 33.4. I guess the key area decision that was made was around, there were originally two projects. One focused on the core biometric capability, and one focused on in-person enrollment, which is the fingerprinting for new refugees and so on. So there were originally two projects, but they were brought together. I think that was in 2020.

I'll check the timeline. And then they were separated again at one point as well. Yeah. And so that was, I guess, what was described in the report as creative accounting. That's exactly what the public service commissioner's review will now need to look into, is how that decision was made, and why.

But how did Immigration need to go to Cabinet to get the whole of life cap increased, was not successful in that, yet still was able to continue the project? As I understand it, there were cost transfers out of the project to allow or enable that to happen.

Well, the agreement of the whole of life cost at that point was 35 million. Yeah. And the total expenditure has been under that 35 million. So the request was to increase the cap to 40 million, which didn't happen. So how did you continue the project at that point if the cap was not able to be increased? If at that point Immigration was saying, "We need the cap to be increased in order to continue the project."

It wasn't. You don't get the increase in the cap. Mm. But the project somehow miraculously continues. Well, I guess that is where the change in separating out- But that was in 2020... uh, no, that was the merger. Oh, the merger. Oh. So you then re-separated them out. Correct. Right. Oh.

Right. In terms of Immigration NZ's approach going forward, is there anything that you can do- Mm... to save costs by being more efficient, taking on board AI faster, doing anything in order to reduce what's ultimately going to be the cost of this on visa holders are both paying for the sunk cost of this project that's gone nowhere and whatever you end up doing, to replace it. And I ask this question as someone who's created software, selling it in the market, and understands that every dollar of cost of development ultimately

has to be factored into the price someone pays. So that's a big concern to me and certainly my constituency. So the short answer is yes, absolutely. Let me go through a few different components. On the biometric capability itself, we're obviously going through the process now of what are the best options going forward.

Things have changed a lot since, uh, 2019. Originally, there's now all of government services. We'll be looking closely at those. So we'll be looking for absolutely the least cost replacement that, um, fits the bill. So that's one part. The Our Future Services program is the major service change that's underway that will be reducing cost in the system.

So this is moving all visas onto the ADEPT platform, which is the modern platform. And that, um, has savings from two main sources. One is cost savings from getting off legacy platforms. And then the second is from efficiencies, which does allow lower levels of staff to be able to complete the same volumes.

So that program is underway. As I said, um, there's been a family of a temp worker on recently, and that's programmed to continue out. So that is a core source over the coming years of savings. Then there is, more generally, focus on productivity. We are looking a lot across all the different visa products and how we're monitoring productivity, how we can improve that. That does go to training, to leadership, to ways of working. Be happy for Ingrid and Jenny to talk more about that. It's a key area. And then as you say, there's what else, what are

the other tools, what are the AI tools, and we're exploring various pilots in that as well. Appreciate there's been some movement in personnel, uh, and appreciate that there is a review process that is going to be undertaken. But in your view at this point , are you aware of anyone who raised a question about the accounting and with concern around how the finances for this project was being managed, uh, that either raised a question or has left?

And in your view, did anyone leave in order to avoid accountability around this project? So I'm not aware on either account. I wasn't that close to the project. In the last week, we have checked our formal systems to see whether any concerns were raised through the formal channels, and we haven't found anything there. But that's, of course, just one source.

So I'm not aware on either of those, but it is what the review will be digging into. Mm-hmm. Yep. Yeah, thanks. That's okay. Yeah, thanks. Yeah, Nick, just following on from Carl's questions. So given you've been there since 2023 in a deputy secretary role-... do you feel personally any accountability in this space?

Look, I was part of the executive team, but as the review showed, a lot of this was outside standard channels. I had limited association with the project. I was aware that it was in a troubled state, and was pleased ultimately when Suzanne decided that actually that was the time to terminate the project.

Hold on. You were aware it was in a troubled state. Did you do anything at that point in your role? Well, at that stage, the chief executive, Carolyn, was monitoring it very closely. She was doing everything she could to get it over the line. There were lots of people, lots of senior people, the executives involved.

I wasn't involved, and I am closely- Okay. Thank you. Metrika. Thanks, Chair. Given the statement you've just made, Nick, about how closely the previous chief executive was monitoring it- Mm-hmm... it baffles me-... that the report was provided to part of the team in March, sorry, in April, uh, and didn't land on the minister's desk until Friday. Those two statements don't match up for me.

Mm. Look, we got that wrong. At the time, I was basically 100% working on the fuel response. So I didn't engage with the report when it came in in early April. The team felt it was better to have a response at the same time as providing it to the minister.

That was not the right decision, but that was- And appreciate you were fully over the cost of fuel and that that was important for New Zealand. But this must raise some serious questions about the next level of leadership. To suggest that the previous chief executive was, in your words, all over it- Mm... yet it sat in MB without the minister being aware- For two months So just to be clear, you're talking about the review, right?

The re- Yeah. So I was in the role when the review came in, in April. Yeah. But I'm talking more as the culture of the organization. Your comment was the previous chief executive had been all over the project. Like this was not- I think the previous chief executive of Immigration New Zealand, I think is- Of MB. Of MB. Yeah, sorry. No. In the last... There were real attempts to get the project over the line.

What happened was, there were defects, as they're called, and it looks like they were reducing them, but every time they reduced some defects, some new ones were created. But this was post this, right? This was last year. The review arrives in April. It sits on the desk. Two months. Until Friday last week.

Banging the table. Sorry. So look, I accept we got that wrong. Yeah. As I said, the team felt they should work out what's the best response before reporting to ministers. That was the wrong call. We've had a conversation about that as our leadership team about expectations of bringing that sort of material to ministers immediately.

So appreciate that, and thank you for having that conversation with your team. Is it going to go further than that? I would imagine if this was any other organization in New Zealand, New Zealanders business leaders sitting out there would be looking at this going, "Surely the accountability is going to go further than a conversation that we got that wrong." And I appreciate the acknowledgement and appreciate you taking it on the chin, but it just doesn't seem like the language and the seriousness of this is really being understood and going

to flow through to real accountability. So look, I've accepted that we got that wrong, that we, and I've apologized to the minister for not bringing it to their attention sooner. I've undertaken with my team that we need to make sure that we are bringing those sort of things to ministers, and that is my response to that.

Yeah. Supplementary, Vanessa Wienrich. Thanks so much for coming along. Look, back in 2024, there was a Beswick report into Immigration New Zealand, and what was disturbing to me at the time about that was that staff warnings were ignored. And we were told that these things have changed, that the culture of the organization had been looked at and you're listening to staff.

And then in this report, it mentions that people who raised issues or attempted to raise issues were moved sideways or moved out, moved on. The culture of that within Immigration New Zealand speaks to a disrespect of staff and a level of not only disrespect to staff, actually disrespect to the New Zealand public and elected officials that they are working with. That they are actively hiding things that are unfavorable and moving people on.

How are you going to address those cultural problems within Immigration New Zealand? What plans do you have to improve or to seek out who are the-- Are they the same people that potentially are working within the organization, or is it a different level of problem? The findings of the review are obviously over the lifetime of the project, which does go back seven years.

So there'll be an element of historic things here as well. But nevertheless, I was really concerned by that. Well, again, we've been talking about with the leadership teams and particularly with Andy, will be starting in the role very soon. Is how do we take this as a time to build that culture?

In all my messaging, I've been talking about the importance of being able to speak up and be heard and we will be doing something on the culture side. We're working through what that might look like, particularly with Andy starting in the next month. So I absolutely agree that we need to do more to ensure that that has made its way through the organization.

Thank you. Just a question on the broader policy work. You talked earlier, this is about injury prevention, health and safety, ACC. For a number of years we've sort of followed along with that. Who does injury prevention sit with? You say you've got the policy teams that sit within MB.
At what point are you stepping back strategically and going, "Where does this sit? Who's taking this? Who's responsible? Is this better with..." How's that conversation going in terms of capability

each other, how we're being really clear about who delivers what. Yeah. Especially, sorry, and just noting today, and the reason I really want to ask this is we talked a lot about roll cages and quad bikes and that, and WorkSafe said, "Yeah, we've made a video. It's really cool. You should watch it." But also ACC said, "We have programs through injury prevention where we fund Farm Strong and various other safer farms and other organizations." So it's clearly in both organizations work being done in that space that I'm not necessarily saying they're conflicting or

competing, but where's that kind of joined up conversation? Sure. So, that's a really good question because there used to be a relationship between ACC and WorkSafe around injury prevention, and that's changed recently. We have got in our branch workplace relations and safety policy that covers three areas of interrelated policy. So it's employment relations in the employment system, it's health and safety policy, and it's ACC policy.

Mm-hmm. I think what we have a really important role in that policy area to do is to bring together ACC and WorkSafe, acknowledging that they each have their separate work programs and slightly different purposes. So ACC is an insurance scheme.

It's about injury prevention and rehabilitation across the whole of the country, whereas WorkSafe is related to work health and safety. But there are areas where they overlink, as you pointed out with quad bikes and roll cages. And what we need to do is work better with them to bring together and make sure we actually have a cohesive approach. And it's not just their public outreach pieces, it's us thinking about our policy settings as well to make sure that we've got them right to be able to enable them to do their

enforcement and their guidance and their education work. And just as a supplement to that, and I'm not trying to be provocative, but a little bit of a chicken and egg kind of situation where like who does the work first? Is there a mechanism for, "Hey, you guys, we're actually already doing this," or, "ACC is already doing this." Do you have that kind of continuity of service, or is it almost a completely third arm of the whole thing where you've got ACC, WorkSafe, and then your policy team within MB, and they're not actually

So you're right in pointing out we don't have a coordinated work program across the three of us. We have tried to do that in the past, and I think it's something that we could do better. We've had a little bit of a success, but we haven't been able to embed it so it really takes off.

Mm. So I think what you see now is we are focused on the ACC amendment, sorry, the Health and Safety Amendment- Yeah... Bill and doing work there. And WorkSafe and ACC, through their data, through their outreach, through the intel and insights, they're getting it going.

Actually, we think there's something here and we're going to do some information and education. We're going to do some guidance. We're going to support people with information. And we're focused here on this particular bill. So I think going forward, there is definitely the opportunity for us to work closer, particularly around, say, injury prevention, where we have those overlapping roles, and make sure that we're more coordinated.

Cool. Thank you. Pamji. Yeah. Actually, I want to go back to the previous topic and want to know, do you know how much of that $33 million, which is basically sunk, went to external providers? Do you know that? Yes. 13.7 million went to NEC as the main vendor, and 5.3 million went to other ICT service providers. And then the remainder of 14.4 was MB's internal and contractor staff.

Okay. So is there a way that you can take some action to recover that money? No. The contracts with NEC in particular was like many IT contracts, where payments are related to milestones that are met through the process. And those milestones had been met. There was a system that almost worked, and it was those milestones that had been met.

It was at the last hurdle that it wasn't, so we were unable to recover because they had met the milestones up to that point. Interesting. So we've got a supplementary from Carl, and then from Vanessa, and then from Tiano. Thanks. And you too. Okay. Thanks, Chair. Just picking up on those numbers. So if my quick math does me correct, there's between 14 and 15 million that was internal MB team costs or contractors. Of that number, how much of that was actual software developers, people really working on the tech versus

PR, management, allocation of overheads, a nice place to park money? Look, I don't know the answer. I'm sure we can give you a full breakdown. Okay. Well, we'll put that as a follow-up question. There would be a lot of business analysts, business side people. A lot of the core software was by NEC, but there would've been some of that Okay. So we'll ask that as a follow-up.

But the follow-on from that is, will you review from an MB perspective, if this project had been undertaken by a commercial entity that had accountability in a different way about how money is spent, whether this would've been more cost-effectively delivered than the spend that's occurred in the way it's been managed by MB?

That's not currently something I'm planning to review. I'm focused on now what's the operating of the PSC's review, which will look into the integrity including around the financials, but then what is the next solution? Well, yeah, given you're going to go into that next solution- That's right... it might be a question worth asking.

So- And so we've got a supplementary. Can we keep them for one or two supps each, because we're going to run out of time. Vanessa. So on the spend on that, 13.4 that was internal or contractor, can you tell us how much of that was consultants? Not off the top of my head, but again, we can provide you- Is that information you'd be able to provide us?

I would've thought so. And how much was spent on the governance arrangements? Again, we'll give you as much of a breakdown of the full 33.4 as we can. Great. Thank you. Right. Tiano Tuiona. Yeah. I was wondering about- The project itself and the expense project interoperability. So was this project going to be able to work with Five Eyes countries in particular in terms of being able to share biometric data with them? Was that the intention?

And if so, how much of the cost was sunken for that aspect? The primary purpose was for it to be our core repository of biometric information to replace the existing system. So that was the purpose. Now it's also true that we do share information with Five Eyes countries, but the purpose of the investment was to replace the existing system with a new upgraded system. So yeah, there was no particular- So there wasn't a specific cost.

So for example, folks won't have to go to the States to see how interoperable the systems would be presumably. I don't believe so. I'm probably stretching my deep understanding of the project, but I wouldn't believe so. And we can come back and if that's not the case.

Yeah, if you could get some clarity around that, that would be... Because it's a big amount of money. Mm-hmm. Figuring if it ended up in Canada or the States or whatever. We have very little time left. We have one supplementary from Phil and one from Pamjit, so if we could round that off. Do you think there's a culture in the department of managing up?

By that, I mean- Mm-hmm... with department manages its ministers and controls the flow of information up- Mm-hmm... and manages processes- Mm-hmm... in the perceived interests of the department. Because that's- Wonderful... I would suggest that's what the report- Says... suggests strongly.

So I don't believe that's a widespread culture across MBIE. Many of our ministers are very pleased with the advice and support they receive from MBIE. We can always make improvements with how we are doing that, but I don't believe that's something that is across the department.

Obviously, this raises real concerns and this is exactly what the PSC's review will look into as to what happens here. So I don't believe it's systematic, but at the same time, I will be, and have already discussed with the leadership team, to the earlier points around the review, is making sure we are always making sure we're fully reporting to ministers and that is an important part of being a public servant.

Pamjit? Yeah. So I want to understand from you what kind of consequences or disciplinary actions are there for any staff or officials, those who withhold red flags. And I'm asking this to understand if those consequences are strong enough or something needs to be done. Well, a couple of points.

From an employment point of view, there are consequences all the way up to dismissal. There are warnings would be at the lower end up to dismissal. So those are the full suite of options available, and that will all depend on what the independent review finds. It'll depend on were there clear breaches of the code of conduct, all of those sorts of things which would need to be substantiated, and then those potential consequences would be considered.

Thank you very much. We've reached the end of our time. Appreciate you shedding some more light- Thank you... and detail on the situation. And my one other little question. Yeah. Appreciate it. Thank you. Thank you.
You two. Two here. Little two in there. Mr. Fleming just joined us. And then- Hmm? Mr. Fleming just joined... then you do name.

To accept the offer to become a panelist. Yeah. So if you could just turn your camera on and your audio on. Yeah. Wonderful. Perfect. Can you hear and see me? Yes. Can I just say in advance, for all submitters, that the committee has read your submission, and please do not name any individuals during your hearing.

But also leave time for questions. We have 10 minutes with you, and you can start now. Perfect. Thank you so much for making the time to hear from me. Again, my name is Tanya Dunston. I'm the CEO and founder of Ponsonby Chambers. My interest pertaining to the legislation throughout my accomplice, pertaining to the safety of New Zealand is paramount, not only as an Australian but also having spent a lot of time in New Zealand, founded my company there. And have become a bit of a resource for people to reach out to me, particularly those who deem to be victims of

judicial abuse or systemic abuse or fundamental mishandling. So the submissions I have primarily are two-part. One is the legislation, because obviously the paperwork is important, but also the practical application of that so it is not undermined, overruled, or easily obstructed throughout court proceedings.

So looking at the Modern Slavery Bill, the primary issue that I can see is the ambiguity between discerning what is human trafficking and what is the modern slavery criteria. So at a point where trafficking ends and slavery begins. And I know with the United Nations' definition of exploitation, it is not as restrictive as New Zealand has chosen to be, particularly removing the financial exploitation of children as a basis that is not legally covered, which is of significant concern to me. I understand the Trafficking Amendment

Bill has recently included that there needed to be an element of deception in order for a trafficking victim to have been trafficked, meaning that if someone was notified that they were a trafficked individual, they then lost their legal basis to have any recourse or safety measures, which is hugely unjust, and certainly not compliant with the United Nations protocol that New Zealand had ratified, albeit not in its entirety.

So looking-- Thank you for your patience with me. Thank you. So my concerns with regards to the Modern Slavery Bill is how it will clearly be separated between the trafficking legislation and how Parliament can ensure that the intention of the bill is upheld and not obstructed. Another issue pertaining to that is immigration has a policy that it will not issue visas to victims of trafficking, and I imagine it would be exactly the same as slaves or people that have been exploited financially,

on the basis that if they are prevented from deportation, they waive any right of recognition of their victimhood. Which I'm aware of because Australian citizens who have been trafficked in New Zealand are unable to receive a trafficking visa on the very basis that they will not be deported if they don't have that visa.

Which is the very definition of discrimination prohibited under Section 21 of the Human Rights Act 1993, or their citizenship. And then when I've had discussions with MSD, which is now up at the Social Security Authority Appeal Board, discussing how people can be eligible for compensation and safety measures, including therapy, for being trafficked. If a police officer does not issue a certificate substantiating the fact that someone has been trafficked, then they are not eligible for any assistance or care. And I'm concerned that the Modern Slavery Bill will have

the same concern, and worse, that victims are fearful of speaking out because people that are being trafficked and exploited on different farms are told or basically threatened, so they can blow a whistle, for example, on a kiwi farm, and then be told that now they'll be exploited.

So they do not have any of the whistleblowing safeguards that should be enforced, which further encourages the systemic abuse to keep people silent. And that is the concern I have with the majority of the legislation through Parliament, that whether it's intentional or unintentional, it appears to be designed to fail the public in human rights for the sake of maximum profit, and that might seem a little bit cynical, but I have- I've been experiencing some pretty significant human rights violations for the best part of nearly nine years now,

and I do not accept the misconception that systems are broken. I believe they are deliberately designed to fail the people, which is why it is important to have these open discussions to speak to people, quite frankly. If you've read my written submissions, you can see the basis of my concern. You can see some of the precedent decisions that I've raised.

Please ask me any questions you have regarding this. I will be making written submissions in the Australian Parliament also for their legislation that is being reconsidered at the same time, and I have concurrent proceedings in United Nations. So these matters will absolutely be brought to light on a global platform because that transparency is essential for public safety, not just for New Zealand citizens, but anyone that happens to be hopefully traveling through or choosing to stay for as long as they want and are legally entitled to.

So if you would like to ask me any questions, please. Thank you very much, Taz. Appreciate your submission. I think it doesn't appear that we have any questions around the table. Oh, yeah, there is. There is two minutes, so go for gold, Camilla. Thank you. Thanks very much, Tanya, for your submission and your interest in this bill, and your support for the underlying concepts.

I just had a question. I know you referenced the definitions section in your written submission also today to us, and I know that you referenced a previous member's bill that had looked at those definitions of trafficking. Yep. That's been, I think, for the majority, incorporated into the Crimes Amendment Act, which is, I think just recently back from the Justice committee. I don't know if you had a chance to have a look at that at all, but if you did, whether you thought that that particular concern around definitions would be

addressed in some way by that piece of legislation. I've only just looked up Section 98D today and can verify that it is not overruled. It still very clearly states what the definition of trafficking is. It is not designed to protect victims of trafficking. And it does not include-- I've received the protocol, which was actually really helpful because it's not publicly available.

It's a 10-page protocol that was signed in 2021 pertaining to the steps that the police and immigration should both be partaking in when anything is reported. I can absolutely guarantee there is no way that that could happen when any reports of trafficking are data converted into information and then immediately closed without any steps of investigation. And I'm concerned that this bill, without having mandatory and publicly available protocol with safety measures to ensure that there are independent oversight, that the same will not be the case with

modern slavery or trafficking. The fact that it is the police officers that can determine how a report is immediately converted and filed without any investigation continues to be a serious oversight and something that, at this time, when you're amending this bill, you have the opportunity to mandate, including the involvement of the ministers responsible for those portfolios.

And I think there should be more responsibility and accountability, because it's not that people aren't aware of it, as per the minister for corruption at the moment. She's clearly been quoted on iHeartRadio, being aware that the concerns are from the people at the frontline. But no one appears to have been fired. Everyone appears to continue to be on the payroll.

And so the situation as it stands, what I would refer to as organized crime, continues. Thank you. Okay. Thank you very much, Taz, Tanya. We appreciate you being part of this process, your written submission and your oral submission as well. Thank you for your patience and my acoustics in the background. Oh, no. Look, it actually did not come across at you.

Yeah, there was no interruption. Oh. All good. All good from here. Is it the cat or the dog? No, it's a baby. Ah, a baby. Just trying to work that out. I can relate, Taz. That was me about six or eight months ago, trying to do that below the Zoom camera, so Yeah. It's fun times. Fun times. So thank you for your patience with me and your compassion. I'm really looking forward to seeing how this bill comes out, so thank you.

Appreciate it. Thanks, Taz. Have a good evening. Thanks. Move on now to our next submitter. Welcome. We have Carina Brown from E Tu. We have 10 minutes with you. Carina, you can assume that your submission has been read, and that 10 minutes includes time for questions. Awesome, thanks. I just want to-- Yep. Great. Thank you for giving us time to have this conversation.

As I said, we fully support the bill. We just think this needs a bit of strengthening to actually make it better than it possibly can. And one of those ways is around the threshold of a large company. At the moment, it's at 100 million. It actually doesn't meet the same. The financial threshold of a large company is 33 million, and we feel that it should actually be reduced to actually meet it in the financial threshold to define what a large company is.

And why this matters is because it's not actually simply endorsing transparency reporting. It is pushing for the law to capture more employers and supply chains where vulnerable and migrant workers are most exposed. If the threshold remains high, the bill may miss exactly the kinds of contracting labor hire arrangements where exploitation can be hidden.

And that's why we think that lower threshold would be a much better figure. And in reaction to what the other person was talking about, that's why we think there should be an anti-slavery commissioner. And that is because that independence of a commissioner can help both legal and policy commitments into practical, sustained measures.

We also have a concern around the enforcement. We feel it's a key weakness, because disclosure without a strong regulatory body may become symbolic. Because the fines of up to 800,000 at 100 million, that's only 0.08%. And so for them, that money- And especially with big supply chains, it'd be worth them actually not complying to actually, when they have severe slavery risks.

And that is a concern, and we think there needs to be that annual statements alone can become a compliance exercise without investigative powers, resources, and credible enforcement. Large entities may treat reporting as reputational risk management rather than duty to identify and remedy exploitation, which again impacts on the workers.

So those are a couple of things I wanted to pick out from the submission, because I think that it's really important that this is actually a workplace issue, not just an offshore supply chain issue. And I think that's what's really important to us, we've got to realize it's not just often people think that modern slavery happens elsewhere but not here, but it does arrive here as well, and we need to actually ensure, and that's why it's great that this is here. And as I said, we're fully supportive of it.

We just think it could be a bit stronger to protect more workers, which of course is E2. That's what we always want to see occurring. So yep. So I thought a quick overview and then leave it for questions. Great. Thank you very much, Carina. There doesn't appear to be any questions around the table, but as you know, not an indication of a lack of interest. You've just covered your point very clearly, very concisely.

So we appreciate you being part of this process. Thank you. Thank you. Kia ora. Cool. We will move on now to Financial Services Council of New Zealand Incorporated. Good afternoon, chairs. Kia ora. We have 10 minutes with you and Kirk, hello.

We have 10 minutes with you both. Hi. How you doing? That includes time for questions and you can consider the submission as read, highlight what you'd like us to be across and we'll go to questions after that. Brilliant. Thank you. Thank you, Chair. We'll crack into it. As you say, we're assuming the submission's read.

Just a couple of key points. There are some definitional issues we think that could be tidied up within the bill. We've outlined those in our submission. The definition of control, the definition of involved in, and some ambiguity arising from undefined terms in relation to supply chains. But there's probably a broader point that we'd like to make. Obviously, we support the bill.

It's great that actually it's a live example of bipartisanship in the parliament. So, good on you for that. One sort of point that's relevant for financial services, and it won't just be for the members of the Financial Services Council, which cover funds management, life and health, but in other relevant jurisdictions, they've taken a proportionate, and risk-based approach to this type of regulation.

Noting of course, that fund managers, life insurers, and health insurers generally have necessarily different risk profiles from businesses operating complex physical supply chains. So that's why they've used proportionate frameworks, in Australia, in the European Union in a slightly different way in the United Kingdom. So, that would be sort of our key suggestions and, maybe, Amy, did you have anything that you would like to add to that?

I think from a practical implementation aspect, the six months timeframe is impractical for many operations in regards to relying for reporting aspects. At least 12 months would be required from that aspect, and I think that clarity is useful to ensure that the reporting is clear for everyone, and also means that people can actually report from that aspect. I also think about alignment in regards to existing financial year, as a entity's financial year is a better aspect rather than having it out of light and aligned to the March

31 reporting as required. Those are the key points I had. If members have any questions? So just to clarify the last comment you made, so aligning it to the reporting entity's financial year? Yes. Yep. Are there any questions from members? Camilla Belich.

Thank you. Thanks, both for your submission and your written submission. I just had a question in relation to director liability. In your written submission, you mention that those acting in good faith shouldn't face disproportionate personal exposure. If that not acting in bad faith was a requirement, would that be sufficient to allay your concerns with that particular provision?

Yeah. I think so. We'd have to have a quick look at it, but I think so. I guess the idea was, particularly if you think about where we're coming from, if you have that proportionate framework, within that and what you're saying is that those directors are not inherently behaving poorly, and there are a range of other regulatory tools that are in place to manage those risks outside of modern slavery legislation.

Then they would have to be very bad faith actors. So again, I think maybe that wording might work. Amy, do you have any thoughts on it? Yeah, I think that's a good point in regards to how director liability works and with other ways the sector is regulated, and as Kirk's point is that if they were not called out in other aspects and called out this, that would bring into other aspects into a question from regulators aspects, whether that's FMA or say RBNZ, et cetera.

What proportion of your investments are in Australian companies that have to report under the Australian version of this? And the question leads to, how close are you to their reporting activities around modern slavery? Just to clarify, Carl, are you meaning companies who have to report in Australia or the investments that are in Australia?

Well, I suppose companies that you invest in, or that you're involved in, in Australia, that have to report under the Australian version of that. Yeah. It's a good point, actually. So just our membership is fairly broad based, so there are some businesses that will have Australian reporting obligations.

There'll be some entities that have other international reporting obligations, actually. So one of our other points is that insofar as they're already reporting to a certain level in Australia- Yeah... if that can be recognized in New Zealand to simplify it, not to reduce their obligations, but to acknowledge that the New Zealand entity is probably going to be a smaller version of a much larger, either Australian or global entity, that will have, again, its own reporting obligations. What we want to say about that is it would be useful to ensure that

those New Zealand entities can have that global connection and obligation recognized. Thank you. Yeah, probably an unfair question on my part, because across your membership, it's probably difficult to know the exact- Yeah... number of those companies. But yeah, appreciate the point that you make.

Are there any other- Some may be reporting, and a lot of insurers have to report into Asia markets or things like that. So there is a vast variation where they would have that aspect that they have to do. Great. Any final questions? Thanks very much. Appreciate you joining us today. Thank you.
And we'll invite our next submitter, Rachel from Fisher Funds.

Hello. Hello. Welcome, Rachel. We have you with us for 10 minutes. That does include time for questions, and you can take your submission as read, but please feel free to highlight the things that you want us to be most aware of. Thank you. Kia ora koutou. Ko Rachel Tinkler toku ingoa. Ko taku tūrangamaahi ki Fisher Funds.

Ko te head of responsible investment. So the key point I want to reinforce today is that legislation must be the most effective, efficient, and proportionate response to the issue it's trying to solve. To be clear, Fisher Funds supports the bill's intent entirely, but there are a number of details in or missing from the bill which we believe will result in the legislation delivering a significant regulatory burden to MIS managers, as well as other reporters, that is not in proportion to the harm it seeks to mitigate and will

distract from achieving the purpose of the bill. So firstly, and most importantly, which has been echoed by the submitter before me, is that a risk-based approach must be applied. So this means entities should build their program and report on the most severe risks and the risks most likely to arise in their operations and supply chains.

Second, the bill needs to be accompanied by proportionate regulation with an identified agency properly resourced to implement it, that will allow reporters to upskill with each report produced. We submit that a phased approach for MIS managers is appropriate while our investee entity reporting capability and maturity develops.

This is a learning from the CRD regime. Another learning from the CRD regime is that sector-specific guidance and its timely assurance is crucial. Guidance will assist reporters with producing reports that are useful to the end user, not overly complex, and most importantly, evidence the work they are doing to achieve the purpose of the bill.

As well as ensure reporters can produce reports without using costly consultants and lawyers to understand their obligations. And finally, I just wanted to clarify a point from our submission. We do support penalties to give the legislation teeth, but believe these should be civil and focus on deliberate non-compliance. This was obviously touched on in the previous submission as well.

This will help reduce costs and the distraction of a reasonable assurance audit that directors will otherwise request to mitigate their personal liability under the bill as currently drafted. And that again, is another learning from the CRD regime. In year one, despite audit not being required, we had to go through a very extensive and expensive reasonable assurance audit process, and that added a lot of that compliance burden to the process.

So thank you for the opportunity. Happy to take questions. Thank you. We have a question first from Camilla Belich. Thanks very much for your written submission and for attending today. I just had a question around due diligence. You've said that you support that but recommend having that at the three-year review period. I just wondered if you could say why you think that that would be a useful thing to have at that point in time, and also just to clarify why. Is it your position that- It shouldn't come in before that because it needs

someone, an entity or someone formally to be responsible for that. I just was interested on your thoughts on that point. Yeah, I think there's a few factors that feed into that. Certainly, a government entity or anti-slavery commissioner being appointed ahead of the mandatory due diligence coming in, again, to provide that extensive guidance for companies and reporters to make sure they're doing it properly.

We didn't want mandatory due diligence to be included from the beginning, and that risked the bill being thrown out altogether, because we really believe in the importance of the bill and don't want the mandatory due diligence piece to inhibit its progression through the system, the process.

I think, listening to other submissions, there's a difference between us being a first mover on this and this being our first move in New Zealand to bring in this type of legislation. And somewhat ironically, mandatory due diligence does sort of actually bring this risk-based approach and proportionate approach that we're submitting should be in there. That comes in through the mandatory due diligence process, as does sort of better clarification on these definitions and things. So, yeah, certainly, a phased approach is still better

than not having it, but to allow reporters to up-skill before the mandated point comes in, and supported by the government entity in charge and the anti-slavery commissioner, et cetera. Thank you. Right. Thank you very much. A supplementary question from Tauno Tōwaho.

Yeah. Thank you for your submission. You had an interesting turn of phrase between being a first mover and our first move. Mm. And I was wondering, just for the clarity of people watching and also for us here, what would you say our second move or third move would be?

I think aligning more closely with Australia should be the second move. And obviously, right now, Australia does not have a mandatory due diligence requirement, but that is certainly the way it's heading. And watching Camilla and Greg's submission to the committee, that sort of timing was mentioned of we've got the Australian Act.

It's very clear from all the international experience that a transparency law on its own is not shifting the dial. However, what it has served is to bring awareness to the issue, and to start to push towards that, I guess, better practice of mandatory due diligence.

So then the second move, I think, for us, should be bringing in that mandatory due diligence as well. Great. Thank you very much. Rachel, thanks so much for the written and oral submission. Really constructive and helpful for our process, so we really appreciate it. Great.
Thank you so much. Yeah, we'll move on to our next submitter.
For our next submitter, we have Alana Chapman from 27 Seconds who's hopefully ready early. We are doing well for time.

Welcome, Alana. We have 10 minutes with you this afternoon. That includes time for questions. Please feel free to take us through the highlights of what you'd like us to be overly aware of, and then we'll go to questions. Thank you. Wonderful. Thank you for the opportunity to speak to this today. My name is Alana Chapman, and together with my husband, we co-founded 27 Seconds.

27 Seconds is a wine brand with a simple message to raise awareness of modern slavery and support organizations working to combat it. Our name comes from an older UNICEF statistic estimating that 1.2 million children were trafficked into slavery every year. And when you break those statistics down, that equates to one person every 27 seconds. We donate our profits to organizations working with survivors of slavery.

And we were talking with an anti-human trafficking leader, Matt Freedman, and he actually said, "You really should change your name and where you are donating your profits. The key change is actually going to be in business and legislation. Those are the most two powerful tools to reduce exploitation at this scale." Which is why we are so encouraged to see this bill before Parliament.

We want to acknowledge the work that has gone into this, and we believe it's a really important step. One thing, and I see the person before me mention this as well, is that we have the great advantage of coming to this conversation later than many other countries. And so we can look at what has worked and what hasn't worked.

And what we are proposing is that mandatory due diligence is added. As you heard before, Australia is a particularly useful example. We don't need to start from scratch. We have the opportunity to take those lessons on board and create legislation that is stronger and effective from the outset.

Reporting is important and transparency, but it doesn't actually protect the people. And that's at the heart of this bill is- Protecting those who have been subjected to slavery. We recognize that implementation should be practical and proportionate for business, and we're not suggesting perfection, nor that business can eliminate every risk. Rather, we support the requirement that businesses take reasonable steps to identify, prevent, mitigate, and respond where they are found.

In our view, due diligence is what transforms transparency into accountability and action. We appreciate that no legislation will eliminate modern slavery overnight. The question is not whether this bill is perfect, but whether we can make a good bill stronger before it becomes law. We're grateful for the work that has gone into this, and respectfully ask the committee to consider adding mandatory due diligence before it proceeds any further.

Thank you for your time. Thank you very much, Elena. Can I just say that a number of us committee members have gone onto your website while you've been submitting to just check out your jewelry. It's always because you've been incredibly concise and constructive with your submission, and you've left no questions for us to ask. So can I just say thank you. Thank you for taking action and not waiting for legislation to come in to do something good in this space.
